Narrative_Meaning: Kings dignify an older mature male, the patriarchy, business acumen, wisdom, knowledge and good advice.

Yarn.Yarn_Description: The Butcher card represents blood, death and carrion, but it also represents sustenance, food and support within a community. The hard part of reading the card is knowing when the card is a sign of disaster and when it means a mighty feast, or more importantly who that feast is for.

Significator.Plot: The Butcher Plot is usually set within a small community, and tends to be about survival, this may be survival against a harsh environment, like a drought or harsh winter, or more normally a killer is loose in the community. This killer is usually a serial killer and a member of the community, but it may be a Monster from outside the community in the horror genre.
Significator.Side: The side marked by the Butcher will have to kill by the end the Conflict. This killing may be symbolic, or literal, and can be an animal, or even an insect if need be, although usually the animal will be eaten (unless vermin) or otherwise consumed. In many cases the Butcher also indicates the side may provide a feast to those who are hungry, or that their services and allegiances are for sale.
Significator.Character:
The Butcher Significator indicates a Character who is a killer, but often for the good of the community.

They Gain Yin when they provide for the hungry, poor, or in need.

They Gain Yang when they care about how clean their hands are (wash, wipe, wear gloves).

They Gain Chi when they execute, or prepare an attack or piece of meat.

They also gain +1 Success Levels to all physical attacks.
Significator.Scene: The Butcher Scene is usually a High Stakes Ordeal where a killer is involved in most genres, but it can be used to represent a party or feast. Butchers can also represent Allies from within a community. Yarn-Tellers can play a Butcher card to kill (or at least summon a powerful killer to attack the target), or to provide food for a whole community, or host a spectacular feast.

Yarn.Aspect_Description: The Hook targets the Character's Hitches, the bigger the better.
Yarn.Hook_Description: The Hook just consists of some enormous danger, it isn't looming, it isn't coming, it is right here, right now.
Yarn.Fray_Description: Being Hunted (or hunting) is a High Stakes Ordeal, that may begin with a Stalk or Chase, that explodes into a Fight. A Hunt is usually a team sport, with the Hunter being superior in power level to the Prey.
Yarn.Snag_Description: The Opposed Embodiment or at least one Force of Nature Monster (with at least 2 Monster Facets - usually either the Dominant Facet and it's Anti-Facet or the Pressed Facet) Extra engages the party during the Fray. At any time except their Story's Zenith Act, the Big Bad is protected by actual 'Plot Armour' meaning that the Plot Demon will Soak Wounds, etc. for them. Powerful Character's can still defeat them (and the Plot by extension) during the early Acts, so Ref's should handle with care, and usually use a FON Extra instead.

NPC.Description: The Butcher may engage in slaughtering animals, dressing their meat, or selling the cuts. Butchers can also be killers and careless surgeons.

Yarn.Sweeping_Text: The Sweeping grants the PCs a huge feast or festival, there may be gains, in the form of gifts as well as food, drink, music and dancing, and there will be meat.
Yarn.History_Description: The Butcher indicates that before the current situation someone or something was killed. This may be an animal, but more often (especially in crime rich genres) is a character, the killing is often deliberate, but can be accidental.
Yarn.Situation_Description: The Butcher indicates that the current situation involves a killer, this may be a lone psychopath, a predatory animal, or occasionally a monster.
Yarn.Solution_Description: The Butcher indicates that the solution to the current situation involves a feast, usually this means a large celebration where people can eat together and the shared experience will allow them to find common ground, sometimes it may imply that the solution is to over-feed the monster or whatever so it falls asleep and can be killed, even less commonly the only solution will be to eat the cause.

Revelations.About_Description: The information revealed is about the most powerful foe the Yarn-Teller might encounter. The Big Bad may not even be a part of the current Conflict, but represent a higher tier of Plot (perhaps even the Dominant side of the Cycle Conflict)
Revelations.Info_Description: How exactly is what they plan to do, going to be done? You may get the full plan or just the important pieces. You might get How to stop it, but not what they do. It all depends on the situation at hand.
Revelations.Vector_Description: Some sweet, subtle details, or a very tricky to decipher clue, conveys the information. It may be the things that were left unsaid in a conversation, a tone of voice or an expression used, but something gives away the information.
Revelations.Alternate_Description: Designs are sometimes detailed plans, other times they are broad sketches, depending how early in the process the designs were made. Designs can also be what the Embodiment is trying to achieve, without even a sketch of a plan.
Revelations.Detail_Description: Salient details are just the most important things that anyone might need to know. "So... We are all going to die."

Obstacle.Description: The way is blocked by a deadly trap and the remains of its last victim. The Trap must be Extreme Stakes and be primed with at least one Mortal or Carnage Wound. The remains will make a one, two or three card psych attack automatically depending upon how long ago the trap last fired.

Tarot.Normal: Cause Fear. +2 cards to Psych Attacks and +1 card to Psychology Tests the Target causes while atop the pile.
Tarot.Crossed: Self-Control. -1 Card from Psychology test (Immunity to Fear) and -2 from Psych Attacks on the Target while atop the pile.

Ordeal.Obstacles: 2 Obstacles in every Non-Court Stage, 3 in every Court Stage
Ordeal.Stage_Description: A fork in the road (two directions to choose from), perhaps a navigation way-point, a place to search, or a chance to spot dangers ahead. A decision point, where you may be choosing between two equally perilous ways to proceed.
Ordeal.Motional_Description: Run! No, across the cracking ice. It is right behind you!
Ordeal.Tide_of_Battle_Text: The Flowing side add an additional Success Level to all Attacks that they make while the Tide is at Peak, all Defence and Healing actions add a Failure Level during this Stage, but immediately after this peak the Ebb and Flow will reverse automatically.
Ordeal.Fight_Description:
The Fight is against a huge Monster, such as a Kaiju (a Location with Monster Facets). This may be Demonic in nature too, or just extremely radioactive or something.
  Facet    Monster  
Gossamer ⚊ ♠

Suit: Spades ♠

Ordeal Trump: Attack / Perception / Penetration (Opening / Navigation / Logic)

Description: The Spades Suit is the suit of suffering, said to be good for the soul, but miserable for a Monday. Competition, perception, endurance and curiosity are positive aspects associated with the suit, but spades also represent destruction, betrayal, distance, might and death. In the tarot, the Suit is replaced with Swords, which (depending on the tradition) may represent either damaging Fire, or more commonly the cutting intellect of Air.

Tarot Meaning: Misfortune, Suffering, Loss, Mourning, Treachery, Betrayal, War, Lawsuit, Conflict.

Ordeal Text: Ordeal Types: Confrontation (e.g. Warfare, Lawsuit), Competition (Sports, Quizzes etc), Perception (Spot Hidden). Spades act as Trumps for the Following Action Types: Attacking, Balance, Climbing, Contagion, Desecration, Destruction, Feats of Strength, Lock-picking, Navigation, Perception (Search/Spot), Staying power, and Stoicism. Spades can be used for any type of attack.

Parry:Redirect-AttackThe damage from the original attack (less the Parried Card) is redirected by the Defender to the Attacker, their Descendants or their Allies.

Soak: Counter-AttackThe matched Attack card is reflected back at the Attacker, or their Descendant, as Narratively appropriate.

Wiry — The Monster is immensely physically powerful, although often its form may not reveal it. It often demonstrates supernatural power as well as strength and toughness. A small, strong Goblin, a robot that looks like a little girl, a cybernetically enhanced rat, or even a killer doll (when not an Object Monster) are all examples of a Gossamer Monster. The Wiry Monster can add Gossamer Score cards to its Monster Pool whenever it is underestimated, cared for, or demonstrates its strength. A Wiry monster may add their current Monster Pool Size Score (Number Reduced) Success Levels to any action with their strength or toughness. Wiries must spend cards from their Monster Pool as though it was a Prepared Defence to ignore damage.
Inertia ⚋ ♠

Suit: Spades ♠

Ordeal Trump: Attack / Perception / Penetration (Opening / Navigation / Logic)

In the tarot, the Suit is replaced with Swords, which (depending on the tradition) may represent either damaging Fire, or more commonly the cutting intellect of Air.

Spades can be used for any type of attack.

Parry:Redirect-AttackThe damage from the original attack (less the Parried Card) is redirected by the Defender to the Attacker, their Descendants or their Allies.

Soak: Counter-AttackThe matched Attack card is reflected back at the Attacker, or their Descendant, as Narratively appropriate.

Unstoppable — The Monster is inexorable, it does not stop and cannot be killed by any normal means. They start play with a Monster Pool of Inertia Score cards. Unstoppable Monsters can add Inertia Draw cards to their Monster Pool whenever they cross a threshold, pass a barrier, or are forced to stop or turn around. Unstoppable Monsters can ignore a number of Wounds equal to the number of cards in their Monster Pool. Wounds stacked beyond the Monster Pool affect them as normal. Inexorable Monsters can also ignore all Hitches with a Boon less than their Monster Pool Size. Whenever an Unstoppable Monster is damaged beyond this limit, it must Discard the Highest Card in the Monster Pool, it may immediately also Discard any Wounds lower than discarded Monster Card, any remaining Wounds are then discarded on a one Monster Pool card per Wound basis. However, while discarding these wounds and cards the Monster must remain still (playing dead) until a Chi Extend Duration equal to the Number of Monster Pool cards Discarded (the initial card is instant, the next discard takes an action, then a second takes a few seconds, the third Wound takes an Ordeal Round, 25 Wounds would take a week). Examples of the Inertial Monster type include the Hungry Dead (Zombies) and most robotic assassins (especially ones from the future).
Key ⚊ ♠

Suit: Spades ♠

Ordeal Trump: Attack / Perception / Penetration (Opening / Navigation / Logic)

Hunter — The Monster is a hunter or tracker, it should favour harrying attacks and hit and run tactics. Typically such a Monster will vanish or flee if it is wounded, only to reappear when healed. The hunter may be fooled by false trails, but never for long. Hunters may add Key Draw cards to their Monster Pool whenever they spot a track, clue, or similar, that indicates the subject of their hunt. Hunters receive a bonus to their Score or Pips equal to the number of cards in the Monster Pool on any tracking, attacking or hunting actions if they also spend a card from the Monster Pool on the action. Hunters may convert Mortal and Carnage Wounds that they take into Crippling Wounds instead, at a cost of one card from their Monster Pool. In T13, the archetypal version of this Monster is a Bulmäs Pack member from the Wild Hunt, but any Werewolf or Animal Shifter would probably qualify, as would robot cowboys in theme parks that have gone wrong, and any fantasy world’s Elven ranger.
Miasma ⚋ ♠

Suit: Spades ♠

Ordeal Trump: Attack / Perception / Penetration (Opening / Navigation / Logic)

Description: The Spades Suit is the suit of suffering, said to be good for the soul, but miserable for a Monday. In the tarot, the Suit is replaced with Swords, which (depending on the tradition) may represent either damaging Fire, or more commonly the cutting intellect of Air.

Soak: Counter-AttackThe matched Attack card is reflected back at the Attacker, or their Descendant, as Narratively appropriate.

Mutant — The monster is a mutant or chimerical creature made from parts of others. This monster type is the oldest perhaps, you’ll find examples throughout the ancient world, particularly in Greek myth. The Mutant will often be larger, and may be malformed or unsymmetrical, with odd numbers of limbs, eyes, horns, or even heads. The Chimera will blend multiple animals together to create a new creature that combines features of all the “parent” creatures, preserving the best of all species. Mutant Monsters start with Miasma Score Monster Pool. They may add Miasma Draw cards to the Pool every time they accomplish a specified goal, such as eating a human, consuming a soul, bathing in radiation, or just growing a year older, etc. The Monster Pool can be used as a “Mutation” Annex, which can be used to mimic the abilities of the Mutants “parent” creatures, such as a spider’s ability to climb, or an eagle’s ability to fly. The Mutation Annex has a Boon equal to the Value of the Number of Cards in the Monster Pool, and so draws cards equal to the Monster Pool Size Reduced and plays a number of cards equal to the Miasma Draw. The Mutation Annex is often used as a Size Annex for the Mutant. Where a 5 card Monster Pool would create a Boon 16 Annex, and could grant a Kaiju Mutant the size of a Skyscraper. 1950s B-movie monsters are often of this type, giant ants, lizards, spiders, wasps, and even rabbits have been used. Classically of course, Cyclopses, Centaurs, Minotaurs, Satyrs, Gryphons (or Griffins), Hippogriffs, Dragons, Sphinxes, Manticores and of course the Chimera itself are all examples. You can also find examples in superheroes, especially those created by radioactive arachnids, or those with an X-gene.
Trial ⚊ ♠

Suit: Spades ♠

Ordeal Trump: Attack / Perception / Penetration (Opening / Navigation / Logic)

Description: The Spades Suit is the suit of suffering, said to be good for the soul, but miserable for a Monday. Soak: Counter-AttackThe matched Attack card is reflected back at the Attacker, or their Descendant, as Narratively appropriate.

Killer — The Killer Monster is a deadly Combat specialist of some sort. Killers add Trial Draw cards to their Monster Pool each time they kill. Killers automatically add +1 Success Level per Monster Pool Size Score (Number Reduced) to all attacks, and Monster Pool Size Score (Number Reduced) is automatically added to the Pips of any attack as Penetration. Killers must spend Monster Pool cards whenever they will make a Trick either with other cards being played by the Ordeal Pool, or other cards in the Round Pool or a Style Reserve. Killers can be drug-addled assassins, crazed shooters, or just steel-souled soldiers.
Yonder ⚋ ♠

Suit: Spades ♠

Ordeal Trump: Attack / Perception / Penetration (Opening / Navigation / Logic)

Description: The Spades Suit is the suit of suffering, said to be good for the soul, but miserable for a Monday. Competition, perception, endurance and curiosity are positive aspects associated with the suit, but spades also represent destruction, betrayal, distance, might and death. In the tarot, the Suit is replaced with Swords, which (depending on the tradition) may represent either damaging Fire, or more commonly the cutting intellect of Air.

Soak: Counter-AttackThe matched Attack card is reflected back at the Attacker, or their Descendant, as Narratively appropriate.

Alien — The Monster is an alien, an outsider. It may just be a metaphor for alienation, or literally be an extra-(or ultra)-terrestrial. Such monsters are usually obvious and inhuman, not just to look at, but in the way they think and behave. They may have apparently supernatural powers and abilities, but often make do with advanced technologies (in the form of additional Descendants). They may add Yonder Draw cards to their Monster Pool when they are observed doing their alien / inhuman / weird actions. They may add Monster Pool Size Reduced Success Levels to any action to do with their alien abilities, technologies, motion or survival (including Dodge, Soak, etc). Alien Monsters must play cards from their Monster Pool to socialise with “non-aliens”. From little grey Eusaiveans, through pod people and pepper-pot shaped engines of hate, all the way to face-hugging space-wasps, there are plenty of aliens.
  Facet    Monster

Lea.Notes: Kings represent paths in the Labyrinth, and Cæster in the centre as well. A normal Bulmäs that tries to walk the Labyrinth would be lost, and would then be recruited into the Labyrinth. The Labyrinth can connect any point in space and time to any other, allowing Bulmäs to consciously time-travel through the Lea.  A King can allow a Bulmäs to create or reload a Chronolith or side-step depending on the power-level of the Bulmäs.

Stress.Description: Stress is dangerous, it can turn a simple mistake into a deadly danger, make a simple shock cause a heart attack, or make a Character hold a loaded gun to their own head.
Stress.Rules: All Spades (♠) Suited Ordeal cards that target the Character for an Attack add the Character’s current Stress as Yarn so 13 Stress adds 66 Pips to each card, but then relieves Stress equal to the original Card Pips, plus all Strain and current Shock (although this new attack is a whole other ball game). Unsuited cards add current Stress as Chi (13 Pips), but remove only the current Stress Reduced (4 Stress), 1 Strain or 1 Shock.

Trauma.Description: Trauma can do odd things to people, making them change their diet, or their behaviour, temperament or even make them hear voices in their head that they can only silence by killing. Sometimes an intense trauma will break a person to their moral core, unleashing a predator, that may appear more monster than person.
Trauma.Rules: The Trauma Relieves all Current Stress, converting it to Chi and using this to purchase a new Hitch, Alt or Monster Facet (the new Alt can also be a Monster if you like).

Age.Description: An Age of Feasting is occurring. During this age a society has reasons to celebrate and is often quite rich, but food rather than riches is how society demonstrates those riches. Vast banquets, lavish meals, bountiful harvests, and fat farm animals are the order of the age. Quite what the society is celebrating will depend upon the previous age. Although the age of feasting is a celebration of some sort, it is also a bloody period, death and carrion are all around, flies and rats swarm the back alleys and sickness is never more than a few neighbours away.
Age.Rules: During the Age of Feasting a society celebrates the last age or its end, the society is food rich due to good harvests or strong trade. All members of society will be well fed, and healthy, at least at the start of the age. Depending how long the situation remains stable, with trade good, harvests rich and animal stocks well maintained, eventually most members of society will be over-fed, and become quite unhealthy, sickness will inevitably sweep through the society, if famine, drought or trade disruption does not end the situation ealier.
